notes: The delta is dominated by format knowledge: 4 of the 6-8 assertions test conformance to the LingBot-World V2 export schema (slots f/g/u/o/space, prompt_en = base+addendum, fixed jump string), which a model without the skill cannot know. All three baselines invented a different format (two produced V1-style WASD hold-prompt worlds, one a custom keybinding schema). That is the intended value of the skill, but it means the benchmark mostly measures format encoding, not prose quality.

The discriminating quality assertions (camera regime correctness, melee-nearby vs projectile-distant semantics, addendum discipline) also all passed with the skill: with-skill runs correctly picked playable vs orbit regimes and pre-seeded nearby/distant action targets in the base prompt, a behavior the skill explicitly teaches.
Weakly discriminating assertion: eval-2 'files written to outputs dir' passed for both configurations; it tests instruction-following, not the skill.

Cost tradeoff: with-skill runs used ~30% more tokens (39k/60k/41k vs 31k/33k/31k) and more wall time (105/259/146s vs 85/119/98s), spent reading the skill+references and running self-verification scripts. Eval-1 with-skill was the outlier at 259s.

Real-world triggering risk observed: baselines gravitated to the V1 lingbot-world format. In production the V1 skill and this V2 skill will coexist, so the descriptions must keep 'V2 / actions.json / slots' vs 'V1 / JSON state machine' clearly separated (both descriptions currently state this).
